text:
90s sports icon Tony Hawk has responded to calls urging him to make a stunning comeback in the sport of skateboarding and compete at the nextOlympics.

Hawk, 56, is the most celebrated skateboarder of all time and played a huge part in putting the sport on the map in the 1990s.

Described as a ‘pioneer’ of vertical skateboarding, Hawk won over 50 major competitions and also produced the first ever documented ‘900 degrees’ trick in 1999.

Follow Metro.co.uk’s live blog for updates from the Paris 2024 Olympics

Such was his status in the sport, Hawk even licensed a skateboarding video game series named after him.

Skateboarding only made its Olympic debut three years ago in Tokyo so Hawk, who retired in 2003, was unable to feature at a Games or secure a medal.

But viewers have enjoyed seeing the American in and around the skateboarding competition at Paris 2024, with Hawk testing out the course before the event started and working as a TV pundit during the action.

Hawk’s presence on TV screens has led fans to call for the icon to make an incredible return to the sport but the man in question says he has won ‘a golden ticket’ with his current role.

Asked whether he was tempted to come out of retirement and potentially feature at the next Olympics in 2028, Hawk toldBBC Sport: ‘I’ve got the golden ticket.

‘I get to skate the course and watch from upfront – it’s amazing! You see people like Arisa [Trew] and Sky Brown, you see the top field in the men’s, it’s anyone’s game.

Skateboarding legend Tony Hawk is delighted to see the sport at the Olympics ð¹â¤ï¸Could we see him make comeback for the Games? We had to ask! ð#Paris2024#Olympics#BBCOlympicspic.twitter.com/1hQYySZB7b

‘I mean, [Team GB skateboarder] Andy MacDonald is here and he’s 51! I’m definitely living vicariously through him. I’m happy because he’s representing you guys but he’s also representing our generation.

‘We went through the formative years, we went through the ups and downs – this wasn’t cool or even a career choice when we were kids.

‘Now look at it, the fact we have someone in the mix that represents how far we’ve come, that’s everything to me.’

In a separate interview, Hawk added: ‘I don’t think you want to see me competing when I’m 60.

‘That’s probably not going to bode well. But I’ll definitely be there as a fan.’

Hawk went on to express his delight at how skateboarding has integrated into the Olympics, though he insists the sport did not need any ‘validation’.

‘This whole movement has been a long time coming,’ he said.

‘The Olympics needs our cool-factor more than we needed their validation but the marriage has been very beneficial to everyone.’

Hawk’s friendAndy MacDonald will represent Great Britain’s in the men’s skateboarding event on Wednesday.

The 51-year-old turned out for America for two decades but switched his allegiances earlier this year and was able to do so due to having a British father.

MacDonald will look to secure another skateboarding medal for Team GB after16-year-old Sky Bronze won bronze in the women’s competition.

‘This journey has been crazy with lots of downs but lots of ups,’ Brown said after winning her second Olympic medal following another bronze in Tokyo.

‘Since Tokyo I have learned a lot. I was one spot away from qualifying for the surfing. It made me stronger. I am super stoked. I wasn’t even sure I was going to make it here.’

title: 90s sports icon responds to calls to make stunning Olympics comeback | Metro News
description: 90s icon Tony Hawk has responded to calls to make a stunning comeback in the sport of skateboarding and compete at the next Olympics.
